groundTruth.title: Case-insensitive path comparison on Unix breaks certificate directory detection
groundTruth.description: The SSL_CERT_DIR validation logic uses StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase when comparing Unix file paths. On Unix systems, file paths are case-sensitive, so '/home/user/certs' and '/home/user/Certs' are different directories. This case-insensitive comparison will incorrectly match different directories, causing the tool to report that the certificate directory is already configured when it's not, preventing proper certificate trust setup and leaving certificates untrusted.

groundTruth.title: Using display path instead of actual path breaks certificate directory validation
groundTruth.description: The code uses prettyCertDir (which contains '$HOME' placeholder for display purposes) instead of certDir (the actual file system path) when computing certDirFullPath. Since prettyCertDir contains the literal string '$HOME' rather than the expanded home directory path, Path.GetFullPath() will fail to resolve it correctly or resolve it to an incorrect path. This causes the directory comparison logic to always fail, preventing the tool from detecting when the certificate directory is already configured in SSL_CERT_DIR, resulting in unnecessary warning messages to users who have already configured their environment correctly.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect event level causes important certificate warnings to be suppressed in non-verbose mode
groundTruth.description: When verbose mode is not enabled, the event listener is configured with EventLevel.LogAlways instead of the intended EventLevel.Critical. EventLevel.LogAlways (value 0) actually means all events are enabled, not just critical ones. The original PR intended to show only Critical events in non-verbose mode to suppress routine logging. This bug causes all certificate-related events including verbose debug messages to be displayed in non-verbose mode, flooding users with unnecessary output and making it harder to identify actual problems. This breaks the intended user experience where only critical information should be shown by default.

producedFindings.title: Inconsistent variable naming in event message
producedFindings.description: Event 110's message format string uses {2} for envVarName but the example shows {2} being used in the export command. The message was changed from `export SSL_CERT_DIR={0}:{1}` to `export {2}="{0}:{1}"`. This is correct but the parameter order in the WriteEvent call (certDir, openSslDir, envVarName) means {0}=certDir, {1}=openSslDir, {2}=envVarName. The example `export {2}="{0}:{1}"` would produce `export SSL_CERT_DIR="certPath:openSslPath"` which doesn't match the original intent of concatenating paths.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify the message format is correct. The original was `export SSL_CERT_DIR={0}:{1}` producing `export SSL_CERT_DIR=certPath:openSslPath`. The new format `export {2}="{0}:{1}"` produces `export SSL_CERT_DIR="certPath:openSslPath"`. The quotes and parameter order should be verified against the intended output format.

producedFindings.title: sawTrustFailure logic may be incorrect

The logic should likely be `sawTrustFailure = sawTrustFailure || !hasValidSslCertDir` to preserve any earlier failure.

producedFindings.title: Expression cache may hold references to captured variables, preventing GC
producedFindings.snippet: private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<Expression, MemberInfo> _memberInfoCache = new();
producedFindings.description: The `_memberInfoCache` uses the entire `Expression` object as a key. Expression trees that capture variables (e.g., `() => model.Property` where `model` is a local variable) will have different hash codes and equality for each different instance, even if they represent the same member access. This causes unbounded cache growth as each render creates new expression instances. Additionally, the expression tree may hold references to the captured `model` instance, preventing garbage collection of potentially large object graphs.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using a cache key that extracts the stable member identity (type + member name) rather than the entire expression tree. For example, compute a key from `memberExpression.Expression.Type` and `memberExpression.Member.Name`, or use a weak reference scheme. Alternatively, document this limitation and recommend that consumers use static expressions like `() => SomeStaticType.Property` or ensure the component is used with constant expressions.

producedFindings.title: Display name cache not cleared on hot reload
producedFindings.description: The `ClearCache` method only clears `_memberInfoCache` but not `_displayNameCache`. When hot reload is triggered, display attributes on members may have changed, but the cached display names in `_displayNameCache` will not be invalidated, causing stale display names to be returned until the process restarts.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add `_displayNameCache.Clear();` to the `ClearCache()` method to ensure both caches are invalidated on hot reload.

groundTruth.title: Unnecessary re-rendering on every parameter update with same display name

This means that every time SetParametersAsync is called with a changed expression (even if it results in the same display name), the component will trigger a render. groundTruth.title: Missing display name caching causes performance degradation
groundTruth.description: The GetDisplayName(MemberInfo) method in ExpressionMemberAccessor no longer uses the _displayNameCache dictionary to cache display names (lines 53-74). Instead, it performs reflection operations (GetCustomAttribute) on every call. Since this method is called frequently during component rendering and the DisplayName component can be used extensively throughout forms, this creates a significant performance bottleneck. The cache was specifically designed to avoid repeated reflection calls, and its removal will cause measurable slowdowns in forms with many DisplayName components.

groundTruth.title: Incomplete cache clearing during hot reload causes stale display names
groundTruth.description: The ClearCache method only clears _memberInfoCache but fails to clear _displayNameCache (line 85). During hot reload scenarios when metadata updates occur, the HotReloadManager triggers ClearCache to ensure fresh metadata is loaded. However, with _displayNameCache not being cleared, the component will continue to display stale display names from cached MemberInfo objects even after the attributes have been modified in code. This breaks the hot reload functionality for DisplayAttribute and DisplayNameAttribute changes, requiring developers to restart the application to see attribute changes take effect.

producedFindings.title: Memory leak in ExpressionMemberAccessor cache
producedFindings.snippet: private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<Expression, MemberInfo> _memberInfoCache = new();
producedFindings.description: The `_memberInfoCache` uses `Expression` objects as keys. Since expressions are typically created as lambdas in component parameters (e.g., `For="() => model.Property"`), each unique expression instance will create a new cache entry. These expressions can capture variables from their enclosing scope, potentially holding references to large object graphs. Over time, this can cause unbounded memory growth, especially in long-running applications with dynamic expressions. The hot reload cache clearing only helps during development, not production.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using a different caching strategy that doesn't use the entire Expression object as a key, or implement a size-limited cache (e.g., using `MemoryCache` with size limits and expiration). Alternatively, document that this is intended for relatively static field accessors and not for highly dynamic scenarios.

producedFindings.title: DisplayName component doesn't implement IDisposable or IAsyncDisposable

producedFindings.title: TestResources class comment is slightly misleading

groundTruth.title: Unnecessary re-rendering on every parameter update with same display name
groundTruth.description: The DisplayName component removed the check that prevents re-rendering when the display name hasn't changed (line 50-51). This means that every time SetParametersAsync is called with a changed expression (even if it results in the same display name), the component will trigger a render. This causes performance degradation in scenarios with frequent parameter updates, such as forms with dynamic field expressions, leading to unnecessary DOM updates and potential flickering in the UI.

groundTruth.title: Missing display name caching causes performance degradation
groundTruth.description: The GetDisplayName(MemberInfo) method in ExpressionMemberAccessor no longer uses the _displayNameCache dictionary to cache display names (lines 53-74). Instead, it performs reflection operations (GetCustomAttribute) on every call. Since this method is called frequently during component rendering and the DisplayName component can be used extensively throughout forms, this creates a significant performance bottleneck. The cache was specifically designed to avoid repeated reflection calls, and its removal will cause measurable slowdowns in forms with many DisplayName components.

groundTruth.title: Incomplete cache clearing during hot reload causes stale display names
groundTruth.description: The ClearCache method only clears _memberInfoCache but fails to clear _displayNameCache (line 85). During hot reload scenarios when metadata updates occur, the HotReloadManager triggers ClearCache to ensure fresh metadata is loaded. However, with _displayNameCache not being cleared, the component will continue to display stale display names from cached MemberInfo objects even after the attributes have been modified in code. This breaks the hot reload functionality for DisplayAttribute and DisplayNameAttribute changes, requiring developers to restart the application to see attribute changes take effect.

producedFindings.title: Inconsistent whitespace in DisplayName.cs
producedFindings.description: There is an extra blank line between the class declaration and the first field declaration, and missing blank line between the `For` property and the `Attach` method. This is inconsistent with typical C# formatting conventions.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the extra blank line after line 20 and add a blank line before line 23 (before `/// <inheritdoc />`).

producedFindings.title: Expression-based cache key may cause memory leaks
producedFindings.snippet: private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<Expression, MemberInfo> _memberInfoCache = new();
producedFindings.description: The `ConcurrentDictionary<Expression, MemberInfo>` uses the Expression object itself as a key. Since expressions are often created as lambdas in component parameters (e.g., `() => model.Property`), each unique expression instance will create a new cache entry. This could lead to unbounded memory growth if expressions are dynamically created with different closure instances.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider using a more stable cache key derived from the expression, such as a hash of the expression string combined with the declaring type and member name, or use a weak reference-based cache to allow garbage collection of expression keys.

producedFindings.title: DisplayName cache not cleared on hot reload
producedFindings.description: The `ClearCache` method only clears `_memberInfoCache` but not `_displayNameCache`. If a developer changes a `[Display]` or `[DisplayName]` attribute during hot reload, the old display name will remain cached.
producedFindings.recommendation: Clear both caches in the `ClearCache` method: add `_displayNameCache.Clear();` after `_memberInfoCache.Clear();`.

producedFindings.recommendation: Fix the indentation to match the surrounding code style - the `InputText` should be indented inside the `div`, and the `label` content should be properly nested.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect type conversion in generic component parameter assertion
groundTruth.description: The test validates a generic component with an int type parameter but uses Convert.ToInt64 instead of Convert.ToInt32 to assert the parameter value. Since the parameter is defined as int (System.Int32) in GenericTestComponent<int>, this creates a type mismatch. The assertion compares a long (Int64) value to the expected int (Int32) value 42, which will cause the test to fail even though the component deserialization is working correctly. This breaks the validation logic for generic component parameters.

groundTruth.title: Incorrect sequence validation for multiple generic components
groundTruth.description: The test validates deserialization of multiple closed generic components but checks that both descriptors have sequence number 0 instead of verifying that the second descriptor has sequence number 1. Component descriptors in a collection must have sequential ordering starting from 0, and this test should verify the second component has sequence 1. This incorrect assertion fails to validate proper sequence ordering, which is critical for component initialization and rendering order in Blazor.

groundTruth.title: Generic type parameter mismatch in component marker validation
groundTruth.description: The test creates and renders a GenericComponent<int> with value 456, but then validates the component marker against GenericComponent<string> type instead of GenericComponent<int>. This causes the test to check for the wrong fully qualified type name and assembly metadata. The type validation will fail because the actual rendered component is GenericComponent<int> while the test expects GenericComponent<string>, breaking the validation of WebAssembly component prerendering for closed generic types.

producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in AccessTokenNotAvailableException.Redirect
producedFindings.description: The refactored code removes the check for `_tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl != null` that existed in the original code. When `InteractionOptions` is not null but `InteractiveRequestUrl` is null, the first branch will call `NavigateToLogin` with a null URL. When `InteractionOptions` is null, it uses the null-forgiving operator (`!`) on `InteractiveRequestUrl`, which could throw if the URL is actually null. The original code had a fallback to `RedirectUrl` which is now removed.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add null validation for `InteractiveRequestUrl` before calling `NavigateToLogin`, or provide a meaningful error message if the URL is null. Consider what the expected behavior should be when `InteractiveRequestUrl` is null.

producedFindings.title: Unnecessary Task.Yield in ProcessLogOut
producedFindings.snippet:
// Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out
        await Task.Yield();
producedFindings.description: The added `await Task.Yield()` appears to be a no-op that was added during refactoring. The comment suggests it's meant to 'ensure we're authenticated' but yielding to the scheduler doesn't guarantee or affect authentication state. This adds unnecessary async overhead and may confuse maintainers about its purpose.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the `await Task.Yield()` call unless there's a specific technical reason for it. If there is a reason, add a more descriptive comment explaining why the yield is necessary.

producedFindings.title: Missing null check in DataAnnotationsEventSubscriptions constructor

While this API is being removed, the remaining code path should be verified to ensure the constructor properly handles null service provider. The diff doesn't show the `DataAnnotationsEventSubscriptions` class definition to confirm this is safe.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that `DataAnnotationsEventSubscriptions` constructor properly validates and handles a null `IServiceProvider` parameter, or ensure all call sites provide a valid service provider.

groundTruth.title: Logout validation incorrectly rejects valid logout requests when HistoryEntryState is null
groundTruth.description: The sign-out validation logic was modified to remove the null check for Navigation.HistoryEntryState before calling ValidateSignOutRequestState(). This causes ValidateSignOutRequestState() to return false when HistoryEntryState is null (since GetCachedNavigationState() returns null), which incorrectly redirects users to the logout failure page even for legitimate logout operations. The original code correctly allowed logout when HistoryEntryState is null by using the condition 'HistoryEntryState != null && !ValidateSignOutRequestState()', which only validated if state existed. This breaks backward compatibility with logout flows that don't use history state.

groundTruth.title: Missing null check for InteractiveRequestUrl causes potential null reference in navigation
groundTruth.description: The Redirect() method condition was changed from checking both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl to only checking InteractionOptions. This creates a bug where if InteractionOptions is not null but InteractiveRequestUrl is null, the code will call NavigateToLogin with a null URL parameter at line 40. The NavigateToLogin method expects a non-null URL, so this will cause a null reference exception or navigation failure. The original PR properly validated both properties before choosing the navigation method.

producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in AccessTokenNotAvailableException.Redirect
producedFindings.description: The refactored Redirect() method now uses _tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl in both branches, but uses the null-forgiving operator (!) in the else branch. If InteractionOptions is null and InteractiveRequestUrl is also null, this will cause a null reference exception at runtime. The original code used RedirectUrl in the else branch which was a separate property.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a null check for _tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl before using it, or provide a meaningful fallback/error when both InteractionOptions and InteractiveRequestUrl are null. Consider throwing an InvalidOperationException with a clear message if the required URL is not available.

producedFindings.title: Unnecessary Task.Yield in ProcessLogOut
producedFindings.snippet:
// Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out
        await Task.Yield();
producedFindings.description: The added Task.Yield() call appears to be a no-op that was added with a comment about ensuring authentication, but it doesn't actually ensure anything about authentication state. Task.Yield() simply yields control back to the scheduler, which doesn't provide any synchronization or state validation guarantees. This adds unnecessary overhead and async state machine complexity.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the Task.Yield() call if it's not serving a real purpose, or replace it with proper authentication validation if that was the intent. If the intent was to ensure the authentication provider has processed state, consider using a more explicit synchronization mechanism.

producedFindings.title: Missing null check in obsolete API removal
producedFindings.snippet: public static IDisposable EnableDataAnnotationsValidation(this EditContext editContext, IServiceProvider serviceProvider)
producedFindings.description: The remaining EnableDataAnnotationsValidation overload doesn't show its implementation in the diff, but the removed overload passed null! to the internal constructor. The new overload should validate that serviceProvider is not null before using it, or the implementation should handle null appropriately.
producedFindings.recommendation: Ensure the implementation of the remaining EnableDataAnnotationsValidation overload includes proper null validation for the serviceProvider parameter, throwing ArgumentNullException if null is not a valid value.

producedFindings.title: Test coverage gap for removed obsolete API
producedFindings.description: The test ObsoleteApiReturnsEditContextForChaining was removed along with the obsolete API it tested. While this is correct for the API removal, the test file now jumps directly from CannotUseNullEditContext to GetsValidationMessagesFromDataAnnotations without testing the chaining behavior of the remaining API. The new EnableDataAnnotationsValidation overload returns IDisposable, not EditContext, so chaining is no longer possible - this behavioral change should be verified.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider adding a test that verifies the new API behavior (returning IDisposable instead of EditContext) to ensure the breaking change is intentional and documented in tests.

groundTruth.category: Async Methods Must Use ConfigureAwait(false) in Library Code
groundTruth.title: Missing ConfigureAwait(false) on Task.Yield
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all await expressions in library code (non-test, non-sample projects) to use .ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the synchronization context, but the await Task.Yield() expression at line 291 omits it.

producedFindings.title: Potential null reference in AccessTokenNotAvailableException.Redirect
producedFindings.description: The code now navigates to `_tokenResult.InteractiveRequestUrl!` when `InteractionOptions` is null, but there's no guarantee that `InteractiveRequestUrl` is non-null in this case. The previous code used `RedirectUrl` which was explicitly set, but now `InteractiveRequestUrl` could be null leading to a null reference exception or unexpected navigation behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add a null check for `InteractiveRequestUrl` or ensure it's always populated when `InteractionOptions` is null. Consider throwing a meaningful exception if both are null, or provide a fallback behavior.

producedFindings.title: Unnecessary Task.Yield() added without clear purpose
producedFindings.snippet:
// Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out
        await Task.Yield();
producedFindings.description: The added `await Task.Yield()` with comment "Ensure we're authenticated before proceeding with sign-out" is misleading. Task.Yield() does not ensure authentication state is updated; it merely yields the current thread. If the intent is to allow the authentication provider to update state, a proper await on GetAuthenticationStateAsync() is already present below. This appears to be dead code or a misunderstanding of async behavior.
producedFindings.recommendation: Remove the `await Task.Yield()` line as it serves no functional purpose. If there's a race condition being addressed, it should be properly documented and handled with appropriate synchronization, not Task.Yield().

producedFindings.title: Incomplete removal of SignOutSessionStateManager dependency
producedFindings.description: The test file still references `SignOutSessionStateManager` in the TestSignOutSessionStateManager class and sets it on the remoteAuthenticator, but the SignOutManager property was removed from RemoteAuthenticatorViewCore. This line should have been removed but was missed, though it may not cause compilation errors if the test class has its own SignOutManager property.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that this line is actually removed in the final version - the diff shows it was removed in the test file but the TestSignOutSessionStateManager class definition was also removed. If the property doesn't exist on remoteAuthenticator, this will cause a compilation error.

producedFindings.title: Missing jsComponentParametersByIdentifier assignment on re-initialization
producedFindings.description: In the `enableJSRootComponents` function, when the same renderer re-enables (circuit restart scenario), the code assigns `manager = managerInstance` but does NOT reassign `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters`. The original code always assigned this variable, but the new conditional logic only assigns it on first initialization inside the `if (!hasInitializedJsComponents)` block. If a circuit restarts, the new manager may need fresh parameters, but the old parameters reference would be retained. This could cause stale parameter data to be used when dynamic root components are created after a circuit restart.
producedFindings.recommendation: Move `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` outside the `if (!hasInitializedJsComponents)` block so it is always updated when `enableJSRootComponents` is called, or verify that this is intentionally cached and add a comment explaining why stale parameters are acceptable.

producedFindings.title: Potential memory leak with orphaned manager references
producedFindings.description: The comment states 'We don't dispose the old manager - doing so would cause JSDisconnectedException because the circuit that created it no longer exists.' However, the old `manager` reference is simply overwritten without being nulled or cleaned up. While this prevents the exception, it leaves a stale DotNetObject reference in memory. Consider explicitly setting the old manager to undefined or adding a comment about the intentional leak pattern for future maintainers.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add an explicit comment or code pattern that makes the intentional orphaning clearer, such as: `// Intentionally not disposing oldManager - the circuit is gone and disposal would throw`. Consider if there are any cleanup actions that can be performed without calling into .NET.

producedFindings.title: Test consolidation removes test isolation
producedFindings.description: The test `PersistentStateIsSupportedInDynamicJSRoots` was moved from `StatePersistanceJSRootTest.cs` (which had dedicated fixture setup with `--RegisterDynamicJSRootComponent true`) to `StatePersistenceTest.cs`. The new location doesn't appear to set up the JS root component registration, but the server startup was changed to always register it. This coupling means all tests in `StatePersistenceTest` now run with JS root components registered, potentially masking issues or adding overhead. The original design kept these concerns separate.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that the always-registered JS root component doesn't interfere with other tests in `StatePersistenceTest.cs`. If isolation is important, consider keeping the conditional registration but using a different mechanism to enable it for specific tests, or ensure the component registration is truly inert when not used.

groundTruth.category: Use Curly Braces for All Control Flow Statements
groundTruth.title: Missing braces in for loop
groundTruth.description: The rule requires all control flow statements (if, else, for, foreach, while, do-while) to use curly braces even for single-line bodies, but the inner for loop omits curly braces

groundTruth.title: Inverted renderer type check prevents circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The condition checking for different renderer types has been inverted from `!==` to `===`. This causes the function to throw an error when the SAME renderer type tries to re-enable JS root components (e.g., during circuit restart), which is the exact scenario that should be allowed. The original intent was to throw an error only when a DIFFERENT renderer type attempts to enable root components. This bug breaks circuit restart functionality, causing applications to fail when users reconnect after a circuit disconnect.

groundTruth.title: Missing parameter update causes stale data on circuit restart
groundTruth.description: The assignment `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` has been removed from the re-enabling logic. When a circuit restarts and `enableJSRootComponents` is called again with new parameters, the global `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` is not updated. This causes the application to continue using stale parameters from the previous circuit. The `DynamicRootComponent` class constructor and methods rely on `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` to retrieve component parameter definitions, so they will operate with outdated metadata after a circuit restart.

groundTruth.category: Test Methods Must Use Arrange-Act-Assert Pattern with Comments
groundTruth.title: Test method missing Arrange/Act/Assert comments
groundTruth.description: The added test method does not contain explicit '// Arrange', '// Act', and '// Assert' (or '// Act & Assert') comments to delineate the AAA phases, violating the required test structure convention.

producedFindings.title: Potential stale manager reference on renderer ID collision
producedFindings.description: The condition `manager && currentRendererId === rendererId` throws an error for multi-host scenarios, but when `currentRendererId !== rendererId`, the code silently accepts the new manager without any validation. This could lead to a scenario where a different renderer type replaces the manager, potentially causing inconsistent behavior. The comment suggests this is intentional for circuit restart, but there's no explicit check that the new rendererId is from the same 'type' of renderer (Server vs WebAssembly) as implied by the comment.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider adding explicit validation that the new rendererId represents the same renderer type as the previous one, or clarify the comment if the current behavior is fully intended. The current logic accepts any rendererId change silently, which may mask configuration errors.

producedFindings.title: Missing cleanup of jsComponentParametersByIdentifier on re-initialization
producedFindings.description: The `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` variable is assigned in the original code but is no longer assigned in the refactored version, yet it's still used in `DynamicRootComponent` via `getParameterValues`. The variable appears to be module-level but is only set during initial initialization. If the parameters change between circuit restarts, the old parameters would still be used.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier` is properly updated when a new circuit starts with different parameters. Consider whether this variable should be updated or if the parameter handling needs to be revisited for the circuit restart scenario.

producedFindings.title: Test method naming inconsistency
producedFindings.description: The test method `PersistentStateIsSupportedInDynamicJSRoots` uses 'JSRoots' while the original test class was named `StatePersistanceJSRootTest` (note 'Persistance' vs 'Persistence'). The method was moved from a class with a misspelled name, but the method name itself could be clearer. This is a minor inconsistency in naming conventions.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider renaming to `PersistentStateIsSupportedInDynamicJsRootComponents` for clarity and consistency with the 'JS root components' terminology used elsewhere in the codebase.

producedFindings.title: Missing jsComponentParametersByIdentifier assignment on re-enable
producedFindings.description: When `enableJSRootComponents` is called with the same renderer ID (circuit restart scenario), the code updates `manager` and `currentRendererId` but does not update `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier`. The new `jsComponentParameters` parameter is received but never assigned to the module-level variable when `hasInitializedJsComponents` is true. This means subsequent dynamic root component operations would use stale parameters from the initial initialization.
producedFindings.recommendation: Add `jsComponentParametersByIdentifier = jsComponentParameters;` before or after the `manager = managerInstance;` assignment, outside the `if (!hasInitializedJsComponents)` block, to ensure the parameters are always current.

producedFindings.title: Potential memory leak with old manager reference
producedFindings.description: The comment explains that the old manager's DotNetObjectReference is not disposed because it would cause JSDisconnectedException. However, the old `manager` reference is simply overwritten without any cleanup. While this is intentional per the comment, it leaves a dangling JS reference to a potentially large .NET object proxy that cannot be garbage collected on the JS side until the module is unloaded. This should be documented more clearly or the reference should be nulled to allow cleanup.
producedFindings.recommendation: Consider explicitly setting the old manager reference to undefined before reassigning, or add a more detailed comment explaining why the reference is intentionally kept and when it will be cleaned up. If the DotNetObjectReference is truly invalid, the JS proxy should ideally be released.

producedFindings.title: Test class removal may lose test isolation
producedFindings.description: The `StatePersistanceJSRootTest` class was deleted and its test moved to `StatePersistenceTest`. The original class used `BasicTestAppServerSiteFixture<RazorComponentEndpointsStartup<App>>` with specific arguments (`--RegisterDynamicJSRootComponent true`). The new location in `StatePersistenceTest` uses a different fixture (`PersistentStateTestServerFixture`). While the test server now always registers the component, this changes the test isolation characteristics and may affect other tests in the same class if they depend on the component NOT being registered.
producedFindings.recommendation: Verify that `PersistentStateTestServerFixture` and its associated startup configuration are compatible with having the dynamic JS root component always registered. Ensure no other tests in `StatePersistenceTest` are negatively affected by this global registration. Consider adding a comment explaining why the component is now always registered.
